summary:
The Berlin Blockade was a Soviet attempt from June 1948 to May 1949 to cut off Western Allied supply lines to West Berlin, which lay deep inside Soviet-occupied East Germany. The Soviet Union blocked all road, rail, and canal access to the western sectors of the city, hoping to force the Western powers to abandon West Berlin or accept Soviet terms over the postwar governance of Germany. Instead, the United States and United Kingdom organized a massive [[event:3353|airlift]] that sustained West Berlin's 2.5 million civilians for nearly eleven months.

The immediate trigger was the Western Allies' introduction of a new German currency, the Deutschmark, in their occupation zones in June 1948. The Soviets viewed this as a provocation that threatened their economic control of East Germany and responded by cutting off land access. Soviet officials calculated that Berlin's limited food and coal supplies would force the West to negotiate or withdraw within weeks.

The Western response, Operation Vittles (American) and Operation Plainfare (British), organized around-the-clock flights into Berlin's Tempelhof and Gatow airports. At its peak, Allied aircraft landed every ninety seconds, delivering up to 8,000 tons of supplies per day including food, coal, medicine, and building materials. Altogether, the airlift made approximately 200,000 flights over eleven months. The logistical achievement was extraordinary, requiring military aircraft to maintain a city of millions through air alone.

The blockade failed. The Soviets, unwilling to risk direct military confrontation by shooting down Allied aircraft, lifted the blockade on May 12, 1949. The episode had profound consequences for the [[event:113|Cold War]]: it hardened Western resolve, accelerated the formation of [[event:2666|NATO]] in April 1949, and led directly to the establishment of the Federal Republic of Germany (West Germany) in May 1949. East Germany (the German Democratic Republic) was formally established in October 1949, cementing the division of Germany that would persist until [[event:2719|reunification]] in 1990.
